pleasure expressed in the countenances
of every one concerned pointing out
with evident satisfaction the improve
ments, and promising appearances
of their endeavours. after all this
and the kind Manners of the Commis^rs
in other respects should I apply for
a recompence it would be seeming to
doubt the sincerity of the Hon^ble Board &
counteract that superior judgement
that so honourably have allowed
me all their merit dictated by
their own experience and wisdom and doubt
their knowing how to reward it accordingly
as tho would suppose it necessary
for Lord Wellington to enumerate his
valor and represent to those benefitted
by them to crave a reward that they
cannot but generously make hast to
give according to their ability. –

the concern of my generous countrymen
as to station not doubting for a moment
but that I should be as generously
compensated. If only for the endeavour
to do good. a saving however really done
sooner and having been the means
of saving a ship of the first rate like
the Queen Ch^te. there should be no scruple
about a reward, cases of common salvage
demand it and this is surely of uncommon
consequence an example that extends
its influence saving and by this time
may have saved fleets for I should
not at all doubt the advantage would be taken
those other ships of the Line that were
known to be in a similar predicament with
the Queen Charlotte besides numerous other vessels
and the improvements to be derived
in every Dock yard for however imperfectly they may
manage it. There is undoubtedly great

Dear Sir

I wish to follow M^r Legges
and your very kind advice it is a sort
of trumpeting ones own fame and
merits where it is so clearly apreciated
and may be bad taste especially in
the present concern I can only say
that it is fortunate that providence
so ordered it that I had investigated
a subject for many years with so
much attention assiduity and not a
little expense that I could see
the mistakes that had brought on
so unfortunate an evil that was still
agravated by further mistakes and foresee
its fatal consequences. this
I rose above all other concerns of my own
as of minor consequence this being

Commissioner Cunningham presents
his Compliments to M^r Sowerby, and
begs to acquaint him / in answer to his
Letter of the 1^st instant / that the Brickwork
has been prepared according to his
instructions to M^r Stone the Master Shipwright
who is of opinion, that it is sufficient to
answer all the purposes of the Experiment
and that the tier of Timber is sound –

Deptford Dock Yard
1^st March 1815

[This page is the outside of a folded letter, serving as an address panel.]

[Stamped in black ink, top right:] Deptford
[Stamped in black ink, top right:] 3PV P Paid

[Handwritten address:]
On H M Service

[Red circular postmark, partially obscured by handwriting, containing text including "DEPTFORD" and "KENT"]

M^r Ja^s Sowerby
2, Mead Place
Lambeth

[Red wax seal at bottom center, impression unclear.]

To the Hon^ble Commissioner Cunningham. His Majesty's
Dock Yard, Deptford

Sir

May I request you to order some Trenails to be
sent to me, as follows, viz. one to 6 in each state or time
of preservation to the most perfectly seasoned & fit for use.
At the same time I should like to have a piece of Oak
three or four feet long & four inches thick, in the most
perfectly seasoned state and if the time of gathering
or commencement of seasoning can be specified, as
well as in what size it was seasoned, it will be most
convenient. I am not aware whether I am at all
authorized to send thus; but you will, Sir, I hope
excuse me if I am wrong & favour me with the infor-
mation. I am, Sir, your very humble Servant
Ja^s Sowerby.

29^th Jan^y 1814

I presume it is of no use for me to come at present to attend
the stacking of the Timber as I suppose the weather has not
allowed of much being done towards a platform.

Deptford Dock Yard 28 Jan^y 1814

Sir,

I beg to acknowledge the receipt of your letter of the 27^th inst and have desired the Master Shipwright to comply with your request as far as may be practicable.

I am, Sir, your very humble Serv^t
C. Cunningham

Ja^s Sowerby Esq: Lambeth

[Top half of page, in a formal cursive hand:]

Commissioner Cunningham begs to
Acquainte M^r Sowerby, that no Timber
has been stacked on the Platform, Pedestals
&c on account of the Brickwork not being
sufficiently firm.

---

[Bottom half of page, in a different hand, likely a draft by Sowerby:]

Deptford Dock Yard,
4^th of Jan^ry 1815.

Sir
I duly recieved your note of the 4^th Jan^y observing that no
Timber has been stacked on the Platform, pedestals &c on
account of the brickwork not being sufficiently firm & I
understood from you in conversation that it was to be stacked
in March. I never thought the brickwork would answer the
purpose & M^r Stone had better perhaps not at all depend
upon it but look to some other support.

I am, Sir, your obed^t humble servant
Ja^s Sowerby

[At bottom left:]
6^th March 1815.

[Address panel of a folded letter]

[Postmark, top left, in red ink:] DEPTFORD / EV / 5 JA / 1815

[Postmark, top center, in black ink, partially torn and unclear:] [unclear: T.P.?] / [unclear]

[Handwritten note, top right:] Not known in my delivery M Watson

[Handwritten note, top center:] Turn over

[Address:]
James Sowerby Esq^r
near the Asylum
Lambeth

[A large number '3' is written over the name, likely a postal charge.]

[Postmark, bottom center, in red ink, faint and circular:] [unclear: UNPAID PENNY POST?]

[Remains of a red wax seal at bottom center.]

Dear Sir

Knowing that any information on the
subject now under your consideration will be acceptable, I
beg to mention, that some of the timber used in building Ships
of War & herein some part of the planks, are boiled in a
Temperature above 212 of Ferh:^t for the purpose of softening
sufficiently to be bent, it may be worth while enquiring if this
may not occasion a partial decomposition of the Fiber of the Wood
or perhaps after being thus softened the Wood may not be
sufficiently dried before being used, you will likely have an
opportunity of observing those planks which have undergone the
operation of boiling, & how far they have suffered in comparison
with others. I am Dear Sir
very truly yours
Tho^s Edwards

Army boat
19^th July 1812
M^r. Ja^s. Sowerby

[Red circular postmark: C / 19AU19 / 1812]
[Black ink stamp, partially illegible: [...] / 17 / 1812]
[Manuscript number in black ink: 213]

James Sowerby Sen^r Esq^r.
2. mead Row
near the asylum
Lambeth
London.

My dear Sir

Your request shall be attended to
respecting the peat and water which I
will order tomorrow. When it is lodged
on board the Vessel I will write, sending
the name of the Vessel, when it goes,
and to what Wharf, in London. An Acre of
land on most parts of Dartmoor is of no
value, and is not worth cultivation.
Some parts are bare granite, the rest
peat. The latter any one may take of
the Prince of Wales at 10s per acre
per year by way of acknowledgment. It cannot

be sold. Many men have been ruined in
attempting to cultivate the soil, taking
leases of it at 10s per acre. Remember me
very kindly to all your family
and believe me
most sincerely yours
W^m Elford Leach.

My Father, Mother & Sister join in best
remembrances.

Woodland. Aug. 16. 1812.

Pray excuse Scrawl &c

P.S. I may not be able to get the peat & water
for four or five days, I shall however lose no
time in ordering it.

(Not used)

My Dear Sir

Presuming on that friendship ^which your late noble Brother was inclined to show me, and the kind mamer of your expressing yourself when recollecting me after so many years after I had been a visitor to him at Hayes, I take the liberty to asking your advice as to my concerns (with which, no doubt you are well aquainted) with your Honourable Board [text crossed out: on account of] from the prevalence of the mischief caused by the Dryrot. As I have now rendered real service in saving his Majesty's Ship the Queen Charlotte from condemnation (which would, in the commonest case, be a sufficient reason for demanding salvage) and in assisting in various communications on the subject for the greater part of [text crossed out: ten] ^even two years without any fee of reward or promise to encourage me; may I request your kind advice as to my persevering! however unfortunately the dreadful state of ^these things require it on the part of the Nation; and however ^clearly I may see the means of removing the evil) to the harard of sustaining ^much so great a loss myself by neglecting my own

avocations with no other hope of recompence than at present I have. I blush at what I have said, least it should be premature, but I beg to trust you as a friend.

I am

My Dear Sir

Your most faithful
humble Servant
Ja^s Sowerby

To the Hon. Legge
Commissioner of the Navy

22^d Dec. 1813.

Dear Sir

Presuming on that friendship which your late noble Brother was inclined to show me and the kind manner of your expressing yourself when recollecting me so many years after I had been a visitor to him at Hayes I take the liberty of asking your advice as to my concerns with your Honourable Board, (with which no doubt you are well aquainted) on account of the prevalence of the mischief caused by the Dryrot. As I have now rendered real service in saving His Majestys Ship the Queen Charlotte from condemnation (which would, in the commonest case be a sufficient reason for demanding Salvage) and in assisting in various communications on the subject for the greater part of two years without any fee, reward or even promise to encourage me; may I request your kind advice as to my persevering (however unfortunately the dreadful state of these things require it on the part of the Nation; & however clearly I may see the means of removing the evil) to the hazard of sustaining much loss myself by neglecting my own avocations with no other hope of recompence than at present I have.

I blush at what I have said least it should be premature

but I beg to trust you as a friend.

I am

Dear Sir

Your most faithful

humble Servant

To the Hon. H. Legge

Commissioner of H. M. Navy.

[written in pencil at bottom:]
Was not sent

2 Mead Place Lambeth

Aug^t 4, 1814-

Dear Sir,

Can I have a few minutes conversation with

you, at the Navy office or where most convenient? to you

I will punctually attend any time you pray do me the

favor to mention.

yours very truly

J S

To the Hon. H Legge

Commissioner of H M Navy &c

[Address panel on the reverse of the folded letter]

James Sowerby Esq^re

2. Mead Place

Lambeth

J.S.

Navy Office
Aug: 1^st. 1814.

Dear Sir,

I shall be happy to see
you at this office on Saturday next
at any time between one & four o'clock,
& am

Y^r. very humble Servant

H Legge

James Sowerby Esq^re

M^r Sowerby

N^o 2. Mead place

near the Asylum

Dear Sir,

I think that you told me
yesterday that the Queen Charlotte
was built of American Timber was
she? — I will thank you to inform
me by bearer and to let me know what
remedy you recommended to stop it
— to what extent has the damage gone.

Yours very truly
H Legge

8 October 1812

M^r Sowerby

The Hon^ble Commissioners
of His Majesty's Navy

[Very faint, mostly illegible pencil writing across the lower half of the page]

M^r Sowerby
near
Obelisk
Blackfriars Road

Deptford Yard
June 6. 1814

Sir,

I have to entreat
your direction as to the manner
you wish the Platform (on which
Timber is to be Stowed) to be laid
as we have now materials for the
Piniont — I am

Sir
Your obedient S^t
W Stone

M^r Sowerby

[This page is the outside of a folded letter, serving as an envelope.]

M^r James Sowerby
2 Mead Place
Lambeth
Surry —

[Archival markings: Red circular postmark, partially obscured, reads "MY 26". Remnants of a red wax seal on the left edge.]

[Annotations in top left corner:]
H Legg
& Stone Builders
Lincoln Inn Fields

Deptford Yard
Wednesday July 28/14

Sir

I regret to acquaint
you I cannot proceed with the
Business of examining for the
Stowing of Timber unless I have
the Honor of a personal Interview

I am
Sir
Your Obed^t S^t
W Stone

M^r Sowerby

[This page is the outside of a folded letter, serving as an envelope.]

M^r Sowerby
N^o 2. Mead Place
Lambeth
Surry —

[Archival markings: Black circular postmark, torn, with "AT" visible at the bottom. Red circular postmark on the left, partially visible, reads "[...]IN[...]". Remnants of a red wax seal on the left edge.]

[Archival annotation, top right:]
Deptford Yard
Morning Afternoon
Recd. Aug 22. 1814

Dear Sir

He waits in the field for the showing of the Timber as now in a state of forwardness. so far at least. or order me to with your approval, if you can make it convenient to pay us a visit. I shall feel obliged.

I am
Sir
Your obedient Svt
W Stone

[Addressed to, bottom left:]
Mr Sowerby

[Address panel of folded letter:]

Mr. Sowerby

Answer to a message from Mr. Stone on the 23^d Sept. desiring to know how to proceed
1814 Sept. 24^th

Dear Sir
I presume we might find some old Ballast pigs of Iron to place on the moor stone so that when you have wood to stack I will come at your notice. am Dear Sir yours most truly,
[unclear: W.S.?]

W Stone Deptford Yd

Sowerby waits Mr S- further direction waiting to the mode Mr S- is desired to stack the Timber - being altogether prepared

Deptford Yard
Oct^r 5^th. 1814

Went 6^th inst and saw two pigs of Iron on each stone
24 Moor^g Stumps 48 pigs of I. W. S. & C.L. Mr Stone -

M^r James Sowerby
2 Mead Place
Lambeth

Deptford Yard
Oct 5^th /14

Sir,

By Commissioner Cunningham's
directions - in pursuance of your request
to him I beg leave to acquaint you
with the Particulars of the Materials, I
have sent.

Trenails - N^o 5 - in Store time unknown
5 - Rec^d Jany 1813
5 - d^o March d^o
5 - d^o June d^o
5 - d^o Nov d^o
5 - d^o Dec d^o

all of which are labelled

2. Pieces of Oak two square 6 feet in
length - I am [unclear: either with some?]
unknown to me - of where it grew -

the Piece of Timber from which they were
cut was received in 1800 - & is as old as
any we have in Store - the Size in
which it was received and Stowed away
was as follows

[sketch: a rectangle with rounded ends, labelled '5' on the side and '10 1/2' in the middle]

If your other business will permit
a visit - probably we should be able
to meet your wish in some degree if
you were to pay us a Visit

I am
Sir
Your Ob^t Serv^t
W H King

James Sowerby Esqr

Dear Sir

Rec^d the Pencils and 2 Pieces of Oak in sound this day and will try to come & see the intention for a platform for Timber soon I beg you to accept my best thanks
I am Dear Sir
Your Ob^t & h^ble Ser^t
Yours very truly,
J. S^y

W. Howe Esq^r

[A list of names written in faint pencil:]

Sowerby
Telford
Salton
Mantell
Newcomb-

[Archival marking, top right: 39]

[text crossed out: on Saturday the Plumbers being engaged & on Monday Eng^d one to be obliged to send off on Thursday, and I wish him to continue the same that may add to in doubt how to give it then]
[text crossed out: M^r Salton General's Son / 16 Holborn]

May 27. 1813 M^r Salton called at 1/2 past 10 to union place and found the [inserted above: Auricularia medulina or polatus hybridusse] had made great ravages in the Kitchen-floor, hall &c the floor had been new laid about 9 months since according to the carpenters Acct and was again rotten through in most p^t the place was so situate as to allow of some certainty of the future prevention in a moderate degree, (I advised according) was to take some inches of the earth from below the joists and incline the level to the Area and again to the cellar perhaps. Allowing a grating for an air course that way only. The joists to be suported on brick with leaving an room the skirting to be replaced by stone [unclear: Malthas?] cement to be carried up the walls as a dado and finished [inserted above: with a small] moulding – also in the passage. The [inserted above: wood]

[Archival marking, top left: 40]

Wine Cellar under the Stairs and Support
may have some of the earth which was very
damp closed away and admitted no for air under
the floor of the Kitchen The Carpenter seemd
to comprehend my intentions I therefore [inserted above: thought] the
instead of 3 months repair being wanted it may
without extraordinary casualties Such attention
and discernment may much conduce to prevent
being serious The Carpenter would
have advised wainscot rather injudiciously and
also some other methods on his
was also hindred to replace the paint
by some fresh sadvice which I
offered from natural motives &c I wrote this
as a memorandum which may shew by date &c
if occasion requires it, that may be the result.

J S

[Vertical annotation written up the centre of the page, over the main text:]
[Sideways: Sowerby] The Carpenter would have advised rather injudiciously and also some other methods on his part was also hindred to replace the paint by some fresh advice which I offered from natural motives &c

Drank Tea with Col^l Montell Wife and
her Sister who are the daughters of M^r [unclear: Hasiende?]
of New Street Shoe Lane

[Archival marking, top left: 41]

Salopian Coffee House
5 May 1813.

Dear Sir

This will be delivered to you by my
friend M^r Salton Secretary to the British
Fishery Society, in whose favour you were
so good as to promise your advice
respecting stopping the progress of
that villain the Dry Rot, who has dard
to make an impression upon his house
[inserted above: union place]
at the bottom of Black Heath Hill.

I remain with Esteem
Your's very Sincerely
Jno. Telford

PS. He can furnish you
with some P^r Volcanic
Eruptions at S^t Vincents
when he was on the public Service

[Archival marking, top left: 42]

My Lord

May I desire your Lordships permission to enquire or
examine into [text crossed out: your Lordships] the dryrot or cause of the disaster
in your Lordships house, mentioned in the papers, as I am
anxious to learn why this evil should so much prevail at a
time when every one seems to possess more information on every
subject than formerly. I might also happen to be somewhat
useful from my experience among the Fungus tribe.

I am My Lord, your Lordships, most [text crossed out: humble] [inserted above: humble] Ser^vt
Ja^s Sowerby
May 13. 1813.

To the R^t Hon; the Marquis of Lansdowne
Berkley Squar
